next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

The hospital looked smaller from the sidewalk than it had in every memory Anika carried. In memory it was only doors and machines and shoes squeaking at three in the morning. In daylight it was brick, a cracked ADA ramp, and a florist bucket of tired carnations by the pharmacy entrance. A banner near the revolving door said SUPPORT OUR CANCER WALK in cheerful orange letters that made her jaw lock.

Ravi checked the time before he checked her face. "Storage closes at five. Billing posts at midnight. We clear it now, we stop another month."

He always spoke in clocks when something scared him. Times, shifts, last trains, payment windows. Anika nodded and kept her right foot careful on the curb. Damp weather made her shin sting in a thin line from ankle to knee, like a wire pulled too tight under skin. She had learned to walk without a limp on the good days and with a smaller limp on the bad ones.

Inside, the air smelled like sanitizer and steamed rice from somebody's lunch box. They took elevator C to basement records, where a woman behind thick plexiglass looked up from a half-finished Sudoku. Her badge said LIDIA CRUZ. She had silver nail polish chipped down to metal moons and a cartoon turtle clipped to her lanyard. A paper cup of fennel seeds sat by her keyboard; she popped one into her mouth between stamps.

"Locker two-fourteen?" Lidia asked, already reaching for the form. "Renewal date is today. If it's not empty by four-thirty, finance posts next month at midnight and won't reverse it. They never do."

Ravi slid over his ID, Maya's death certificate cop...
open_hooks_to_carry: First PT session exposes compensations and fear of rest.
Ravi argues with Tess over pace and authority.
prev_tail_excerpt:
He did not argue. He only nodded once, tired and unwilling to concede fully, and handed the notebook back.

"We'll figure it out," he said.

It was the kind of sentence adults used when they had no plan and no spare strength and wanted the room to stop hurting.

That night, after he went to bed, Anika sat cross-legged on top of her blankets with the old phone in her lap and the notebook open beside her.

She pressed play.

Maya said: middle shelf, spoon of water, mustard seeds, stubborn tomorrow, I love you.

Anika whispered each line in perfect time, her mouth shaping the words before the speaker did. She could have done it without the recording. She could have done it in the shower, on the bus, in math class, with the phone dead in her pocket. The recording was no longer information. It was dosage.

At 1:41 a.m., she wrote the final entry for the week.

Night 7
sleep: not yet
wake episodes: n/a
replay count: 19
panic peak: 8/10 then 3/10
shin ache at rest: 2/10

Then, on a line by itself:

I can say her exactly.

She closed the notebook, set the phone face down, and lay back without turning the lamp off. Light pooled on the ceiling. In the next room Ravi coughed once, rolled in bed, went quiet.

Anika listened for sleep and found only the familiar minute-click of Maya's watch against her wrist, counting forward whether she wanted it to or not.
state_deltas_required: Retain eligibility by complying with rehabilitation protocol.
Program officials threaten suspension for noncompliance, reducing Ravi's unilateral authority and giving Anika institutional backing.
